exp_L1_00.label: Diagnostic Accuracy & Reliability
exp_L1_00.description: Concerns about whether AI tools actually help us make better, safer diagnoses—or if they introduce new errors or blind spots.

exp_L2_00_00.label: False Positives/Negatives
exp_L2_00_00.description: AI systems that cry wolf too often or miss real pathology entirely.

exp_leaf_00_00_00.label: Too many false alarms on benign findings
exp_leaf_00_00_00.description: The AI keeps flagging harmless lung nodules or breast calcifications as suspicious, and I end up chasing ghosts all day.

exp_leaf_00_00_01.label: Misses subtle but critical pathologies
exp_leaf_00_00_01.description: It’s great at spotting big tumors, but completely glosses over early signs of pneumothorax or subtle fractures—things I’d catch with a second glance.

exp_leaf_00_00_02.label: Inconsistent performance across body regions
exp_leaf_00_00_02.description: Works like a charm on chest X-rays but falls apart on wrist films or pelvic MRIs—it’s not one-size-fits-all.

exp_leaf_00_00_03.label: Struggles with rare diseases
exp_leaf_00_00_03.description: If it hasn’t seen 10,000 examples of something ultra-rare, it just shrugs—and so do I, because now I’m doubting my own eyes.

exp_L2_00_01.label: Overreliance & Deskilling
exp_L2_00_01.description: Worry that leaning too much on AI will erode our diagnostic instincts over time.

exp_leaf_00_01_00.label: Junior radiologists stop looking critically
exp_leaf_00_01_00.description: I’ve seen residents just accept the AI overlay without even checking the raw images—they’re outsourcing their judgment before they’ve built it.

exp_leaf_00_01_01.label: Loss of pattern recognition intuition
exp_leaf_00_01_01.description: When you constantly defer to the algorithm, you start forgetting how to read the ‘texture’ of disease—the gestalt that comes from years of seeing thousands of cases.

exp_leaf_00_01_02.label: Hard to override when AI is wrong but confident
exp_leaf_00_01_02.description: The system gives a 98% probability on something clearly wrong, and now I have to justify why I’m ignoring it—even though I know it’s garbage.

exp_L2_00_02.label: Ground Truth Ambiguity
exp_L2_00_02.description: AI assumes there’s always a single right answer—but in real life, imaging interpretation is often probabilistic or consensus-based.

exp_leaf_00_02_00.label: AI doesn’t understand diagnostic uncertainty
exp_leaf_00_02_00.description: It forces binary decisions on cases where even experts would say ‘maybe’ or ‘follow-up needed’—medicine isn’t always black and white.

exp_leaf_00_02_01.label: Disagrees with subspecialist consensus
exp_leaf_00_02_01.description: The AI calls it negative, but three MSK radiologists would call it positive—it’s trained on general labels, not nuanced expert disagreement.

exp_leaf_00_02_02.label: Can’t handle evolving standards
exp_leaf_00_02_02.description: Guidelines change—like what size nodule needs biopsy—but the model is frozen in last year’s textbook.

exp_L1_01.label: Workflow Integration & Usability
exp_L1_01.description: How AI fits (or doesn’t fit) into the daily grind of reading studies, dictating reports, and managing caseloads.

exp_L2_01_00.label: Alert Fatigue & Interface Clutter
exp_L2_01_00.description: Too many pop-ups, overlays, or suggestions that slow me down instead of helping.

exp_leaf_01_00_00.label: AI overlays obscure anatomy
exp_leaf_01_00_00.description: The bounding boxes and heatmaps cover up critical structures—I literally can’t see what’s underneath the red blob.

exp_leaf_01_00_01.label: Constant interruptions during reads
exp_leaf_01_00_01.description: Every third case triggers a ‘review needed’ alert, and half are junk—it breaks my concentration rhythm.

exp_leaf_01_00_02.label: No way to silence low-yield alerts
exp_leaf_01_00_02.description: I can’t tell the system, ‘Stop bugging me about incidental renal cysts—they’re always benign in this population.’

exp_L2_01_01.label: Speed vs. Thoroughness Trade-offs
exp_L2_01_01.description: Pressure to use AI to read faster—but at what cost to quality?

exp_leaf_01_01_00.label: Feels rushed when AI pushes rapid reads
exp_leaf_01_01_00.description: Management says, ‘You’re using AI, so you should do 20% more studies’—but I still need time to verify its work.

exp_leaf_01_01_01.label: Double-checking takes longer than doing it myself
exp_leaf_01_01_01.description: Sometimes it’s faster to just read the case from scratch than to figure out why the AI marked something weird.

exp_leaf_01_01_02.label: Emergency vs. routine settings need different AI behavior
exp_leaf_01_01_02.description: In trauma, I need fast triage; in screening, I need meticulous detail—but the same AI tries to do both and does neither well.

exp_L2_01_02.label: Interoperability & System Lock-in
exp_L2_01_02.description: AI tools that don’t play nice with our existing PACS, RIS, or voice recognition systems.

exp_leaf_01_02_00.label: Requires switching between windows
exp_leaf_01_02_00.description: I have to alt-tab out of PACS to check the AI dashboard—every time. It’s like working with one hand tied behind my back.

exp_leaf_01_02_01.label: Vendor-specific models don’t transfer
exp_leaf_01_02_01.description: We bought an AI for stroke detection from Vendor A, but it won’t run on Vendor B’s scanners—we’re stuck in a walled garden.

exp_L1_02.label: Legal, Ethical & Regulatory Risk
exp_L1_02.description: Who’s liable when AI misses something—or causes harm through overconfidence?

exp_L2_02_00.label: Liability Ambiguity
exp_L2_02_00.description: Unclear whether the radiologist, hospital, or vendor is on the hook if AI contributes to a misdiagnosis.

exp_leaf_02_00_00.label: ‘Black box’ defense doesn’t hold up in court
exp_leaf_02_00_00.description: If I’m sued, saying ‘the AI told me to’ won’t fly—but I also can’t explain how it reached that conclusion.

exp_leaf_02_00_01.label: Documentation burden increases
exp_leaf_02_00_01.description: Now I have to explicitly note in every report whether I agreed or disagreed with the AI—adding minutes to each case.

exp_L2_02_01.label: Regulatory Lag & Validation Gaps
exp_L2_02_01.description: FDA clearance doesn’t guarantee real-world performance, and updates happen without revalidation.

exp_leaf_02_01_00.label: Models drift silently after deployment
exp_leaf_02_01_00.description: The algorithm worked fine at launch, but six months later—after scanner upgrades or protocol changes—it’s degrading, and no one notices.

exp_leaf_02_01_01.label: Cleared ≠ clinically validated
exp_leaf_02_01_01.description: Just because the FDA says it’s safe doesn’t mean it improves outcomes in *our* patient population—it might even hurt them.

exp_L2_02_02.label: Bias & Health Equity Concerns
exp_L2_02_02.description: AI trained mostly on data from academic centers may fail on diverse or underserved populations.

exp_leaf_02_02_00.label: Poor performance on non-Caucasian skin or body habitus
exp_leaf_02_02_00.description: The dermatology AI was trained on light skin, and the body composition models assume BMI <35—so it stumbles on our real patient mix.

exp_leaf_02_02_01.label: Reinforces existing disparities
exp_leaf_02_02_01.description: If the AI undercalls disease in certain groups, and we trust it, we’ll miss more cases in those populations—making gaps worse, not better.

exp_L1_03.label: Data Quality & Governance
exp_L1_03.description: Garbage in, gospel out—AI treats flawed or incomplete data as truth.

exp_L2_03_00.label: Training Data Limitations
exp_L2_03_00.description: Models trained on clean, curated datasets that don’t reflect messy real-world imaging.

exp_leaf_03_00_00.label: Trained on perfect scans, fails on motion artifacts
exp_leaf_03_00_00.description: Real patients move, breathe, or can’t hold still—but the AI only saw pristine research-grade images during training.

exp_leaf_03_00_01.label: Label noise from inconsistent prior reads
exp_leaf_03_00_01.description: The ‘ground truth’ used to train the model came from overworked residents or outdated reports—so the AI learned wrong patterns.

exp_L2_03_01.label: Local Data Drift
exp_L2_03_01.description: Our protocols, equipment, and patient mix differ from the training environment.

exp_leaf_03_01_00.label: Scanner manufacturer differences break models
exp_leaf_03_01_00.description: Switched from Siemens to GE last year, and now the lung nodule detector thinks everything’s a nodule—texture and noise profiles are totally different.

exp_leaf_03_01_01.label: Contrast timing variations confuse AI
exp_leaf_03_01_01.description: If the tech injects contrast 5 seconds late, the AI misreads enhancement patterns—it expects textbook timing.

exp_L2_03_02.label: Data Privacy & Sharing Barriers
exp_L2_03_02.description: Can’t easily share or pool data to improve models due to HIPAA, IRB, or institutional politics.

exp_leaf_03_02_00.label: Can’t fine-tune models on local data
exp_leaf_03_02_00.description: We’d love to adapt the AI to our ER population, but legal says no sharing—even anonymized—so we’re stuck with generic performance.

exp_L1_04.label: Team Dynamics & Training
exp_L1_04.description: How AI changes collaboration, education, and role definitions across radiology teams.

exp_L2_04_00.label: Role Confusion & Responsibility Shifts
exp_L2_04_00.description: Who owns the final call when technologists, AI, and radiologists all ‘see’ something?

exp_leaf_04_00_00.label: Techs start second-guessing based on AI previews
exp_leaf_04_00_00.description: Now the MRI tech says, ‘The AI flagged something—should we rescan?’ before I’ve even seen the study. It’s muddying the chain of command.

exp_leaf_04_00_01.label: Referring physicians demand AI explanations
exp_leaf_04_00_01.description: The ER doc asks, ‘Why didn’t the AI catch this bleed?’—as if it’s a co-diagnoser, not a tool. Now I’m defending software I didn’t build.

exp_L2_04_01.label: Education & Skill Development
exp_L2_04_01.description: How to train the next generation when AI handles routine cases.

exp_leaf_04_01_00.label: Residents see fewer ‘classic’ cases
exp_leaf_04_01_00.description: AI filters out obvious pneumonias or fractures for junior reads, so trainees never build foundational pattern libraries.

exp_leaf_04_01_01.label: No curriculum for AI critique
exp_leaf_04_01_01.description: We teach anatomy and pathology, but not how to interrogate an AI’s confidence map or spot its failure modes.

exp_L2_04_02.label: Trust Calibration Across Experience Levels
exp_L2_04_02.description: Novices trust AI too much; veterans trust it too little—and both are problematic.

exp_leaf_04_02_00.label: Experienced radiologists dismiss useful AI cues
exp_leaf_04_02_00.description: My senior partner ignores all AI output because ‘it’s just marketing fluff’—even when it correctly flags a tiny PE he almost missed.

exp_leaf_04_02_01.label: New grads can’t distinguish good vs. bad AI advice
exp_leaf_04_02_01.description: They treat every AI suggestion as gospel because they haven’t seen enough failures yet to develop healthy skepticism.

res_L1_00.label: Expert Mental Models of Discovery
res_L1_00.description: Understanding how domain experts conceptualize and narrate the process of discovering new cell states, including their definitions of 'discovery' and key decision points.

res_L2_00_00.label: Discovery Workflow Structure
res_L2_00_00.description: How experts decompose the end-to-end cell state discovery pipeline into discrete steps and transitions.

res_leaf_00_00_00.label: Map the canonical sequence of steps from raw data to confirmed cell state
res_leaf_00_00_00.description: Elicit a detailed, step-by-step account of the typical workflow—from data ingestion to validation—to identify common phases and variations across experts.

res_leaf_00_00_01.label: Identify the moment perceived as 'true discovery'
res_leaf_00_00_01.description: Determine at which point in the pipeline experts feel they have genuinely discovered a new cell state (e.g., during clustering, validation, or biological interpretation).

res_leaf_00_00_02.label: Characterize confidence distribution across workflow stages
res_leaf_00_00_02.description: Understand which steps experts feel most/least confident in, and whether uncertainty is localized or pervasive.

res_L2_00_01.label: Tacit Knowledge and Heuristics
res_L2_00_01.description: Uncover informal rules, intuitions, and experience-based judgments that guide decisions but are hard to formalize.

res_leaf_00_01_00.label: Elicit unspoken 'experience rules' used in cluster interpretation
res_leaf_00_01_00.description: Capture heuristics experts apply when evaluating whether a cluster represents a novel cell state, especially those they cannot easily articulate or codify.

res_leaf_00_01_01.label: Identify signals prioritized during initial cluster inspection
res_leaf_00_01_01.description: Determine which evidence types (e.g., marker genes, spatial context, pathway enrichment) experts attend to first and why.

res_leaf_00_01_02.label: Understand criteria for 'credible' cell state claims
res_leaf_00_01_02.description: Explore what combination of evidence (e.g., wet-lab validation, replication) constitutes sufficient proof for a new cell state in the expert’s view.

res_L1_01.label: Decision-Making Under Uncertainty
res_L1_01.description: Investigating how experts navigate ambiguity, make judgment calls, and revise hypotheses during cell state identification.

res_L2_01_00.label: Resolution and Granularity Choices
res_L2_01_00.description: How experts decide on clustering parameters and interpret granularity trade-offs.

res_leaf_01_00_00.label: Understand how clustering resolution is selected
res_leaf_01_00_00.description: Explore the rationale behind choosing specific resolution parameters—whether driven by biological expectation, data-driven metrics, or iterative tuning.

res_leaf_01_00_01.label: Identify triggers for suspecting a novel cell state
res_leaf_01_00_01.description: Determine what patterns or anomalies prompt experts to consider a cluster as potentially representing a new biological state.

res_L2_01_01.label: Iterative Refinement Practices
res_L2_01_01.description: How experts adjust clusters or reframe interpretations when initial hypotheses fail.

res_leaf_01_01_00.label: Document strategies for cluster adjustment
res_leaf_01_01_00.description: Capture how experts modify clustering (e.g., re-clustering subsets, merging/splitting) when a cluster appears biologically implausible.

res_leaf_01_01_01.label: Assess tolerance for ambiguous or transitional states
res_leaf_01_01_01.description: Understand how experts handle continuous spectra or intermediate states that resist discrete categorization.

res_L1_02.label: Tool Use and Interaction Friction
res_L1_02.description: Examining how current computational tools support or hinder the discovery process, including usability pain points.

res_L2_02_00.label: Tool Ecosystem and Customization
res_L2_02_00.description: What software platforms experts rely on and how they extend or adapt them.

res_leaf_02_00_00.label: Inventory primary analysis and visualization tools
res_leaf_02_00_00.description: Identify which tools (e.g., Seurat, Scanpy, custom scripts) are central to the workflow and for which tasks.

res_leaf_02_00_01.label: Map tool strengths and limitations per task
res_leaf_02_00_01.description: Determine which parts of the tools experts find intuitive versus cumbersome, especially for critical decision-making steps.

res_L2_02_01.label: Cognitive and Operational Load
res_L2_02_01.description: Where in the tool-mediated workflow do experts experience high cognitive demand or repetitive effort.

res_leaf_02_01_00.label: Identify unavoidable but burdensome tasks
res_leaf_02_01_00.description: Pinpoint steps that are tedious or error-prone yet necessary, such as manual annotation or cross-tool data transfer.

res_leaf_02_01_01.label: Assess need for integrated validation support
res_leaf_02_01_01.description: Evaluate whether current tools adequately support linking computational findings to external validation evidence (e.g., spatial, perturbation).

res_L1_03.label: Epistemic Criteria for Biological Credibility
res_L1_03.description: Understanding what constitutes sufficient evidence for a cell state to be accepted as real or novel in the expert’s scientific framework.

res_L2_03_00.label: Evidence Hierarchy and Validation Pathways
res_L2_03_00.description: How different types of evidence are weighted and combined to establish credibility.

res_leaf_03_00_00.label: Rank evidentiary sources by perceived reliability
res_leaf_03_00_00.description: Compare the relative importance experts assign to marker genes, pathway enrichment, spatial consistency, dataset replication, and wet-lab validation.

res_leaf_03_00_01.label: Determine minimal validation requirements
res_leaf_03_00_01.description: Establish what combination of evidence is considered the 'floor' for publishing or acting on a new cell state hypothesis.

res_L2_03_01.label: Handling Negative or Ambiguous Evidence
res_L2_03_01.description: How experts respond when clusters lack clear markers or contradict prior knowledge.

res_leaf_03_01_00.label: Explore responses to non-confirmatory clusters
res_leaf_03_01_00.description: Understand how experts decide whether to discard, reinterpret, or investigate further when a cluster lacks strong supporting evidence.

res_L1_04.label: Pain Points and Opportunity Areas
res_L1_04.description: Synthesizing overarching challenges that impede efficient or confident discovery.

res_L2_04_00.label: Primary Sources of Difficulty
res_L2_04_00.description: Categorizing the root causes of frustration or inefficiency in the discovery process.

res_leaf_04_00_00.label: Identify the single hardest aspect of cell state discovery
res_leaf_04_00_00.description: Determine whether the greatest challenge lies in biological interpretation, managing uncertainty, iterative refinement, or tool limitations.

res_leaf_04_00_01.label: Assess alignment between computational output and biological reasoning
res_leaf_04_00_01.description: Evaluate whether current visualizations and outputs match how experts reason about cell identity and function.

aligned_path_expert: Too many false alarms on benign findings

conflict.expert_branch: Too many false alarms on benign findings
conflict.researcher_branch: Explore responses to non-confirmatory clusters

assistance_payload.validate_focus: Your focus on reducing false alarms from AI—like unnecessary alerts on benign lung nodules or breast calcifications—is critically important for maintaining clinical efficiency and preventing burnout from chasing non-urgent findings.

pivot.limitation: Relying solely on alarm suppression or threshold tuning may cause you to miss subtle but meaningful patterns that don’t fit classic diagnostic criteria, especially when AI outputs are ambiguous rather than clearly false.
pivot.research_goal: Explore responses to non-confirmatory clusters—understanding how experts decide whether to discard, reinterpret, or investigate further when evidence is weak or conflicting.
pivot.compelling_reason: This approach helps you distinguish between true noise and biologically plausible signals that deserve a second look, turning ambiguous alerts into actionable insights without increasing your workload.
pivot.coarse_scenario: On a typical day, instead of dismissing every borderline finding as another false alarm, you quickly triage AI outputs using intuitive cues refined through this research—focusing only on the handful of ambiguous cases that actually warrant deeper review. Your diagnostic confidence grows because you’re no longer guessing whether you’ve missed something important among the noise.

conflict.researcher_branch: Identify unavoidable but burdensome tasks

analogy.source_concept: No way to silence low-yield alerts

structural_mapping.inputs: Repetitive, non-actionable AI outputs (e.g., benign findings flagged as urgent)
structural_mapping.logic: System forces review of every alert regardless of clinical relevance or user context
structural_mapping.outputs: User burden from unnecessary cognitive engagement with low-value information

analogy.explanation: Just as unfilterable alerts force radiologists to process irrelevant notifications, the researcher’s 'unavoidable but burdensome tasks' capture necessary yet inefficient steps—like manual corrections of AI annotations—that consume time without adding diagnostic value, both stemming from rigid system design that ignores user workflow.

assistance_payload.scenario: During a lung nodule read, the AI overlays a heatmap that obscures the fissure anatomy. The researcher would catalog the extra step of mentally reconstructing hidden structures as a burdensome but unavoidable task. Yet the expert sees this not as a procedural chore but as an immediate perceptual failure—the overlay itself is the problem, not the workaround.

aligned_path_expert: ‘Black box’ defense doesn’t hold up in court
aligned_path_researcher: Rank evidentiary sources by perceived reliability

conflict.expert_branch: ‘Black box’ defense doesn’t hold up in court
conflict.researcher_branch: Rank evidentiary sources by perceived reliability

assistance_payload.validate_focus: Your focus on the legal vulnerability of relying on unexplainable AI in clinical decisions is highly relevant—clinicians can’t use 'the AI told me to' as a defense if they can’t justify or understand the reasoning behind a diagnosis.

pivot.limitation: Relying solely on AI outputs without a structured way to assess the reliability of the underlying evidence leaves clinicians exposed not just legally, but also clinically, when recommendations lack biological plausibility or reproducibility.
pivot.research_goal: Rank evidentiary sources by perceived reliability—such as marker genes, pathway enrichment, spatial consistency, dataset replication, and wet-lab validation—to establish a defensible hierarchy of scientific support for AI-generated insights.
pivot.compelling_reason: By anchoring AI recommendations in a transparent, consensus-based evidence hierarchy, you gain a credible, court-ready rationale that aligns clinical judgment with scientifically validated reasoning—turning opaque outputs into defensible medical decisions.
pivot.coarse_scenario: On a typical day, a clinician receives an AI-suggested diagnosis but can immediately reference its supporting evidence tier—knowing it’s backed by replicated datasets and wet-lab validation. During a peer review or legal inquiry, they confidently explain not just what the AI recommended, but why it’s scientifically trustworthy.

aligned_path_expert: Trained on perfect scans, fails on motion artifacts
aligned_path_researcher: Assess alignment between computational output and biological reasoning

conflict.expert_branch: Trained on perfect scans, fails on motion artifacts
conflict.researcher_branch: Assess alignment between computational output and biological reasoning

analogy.source_concept: Label noise from inconsistent prior reads

structural_mapping.inputs: Noisy or inconsistent diagnostic labels from human readers
structural_mapping.logic: AI learns spurious correlations based on flawed ground truth
structural_mapping.outputs: Misclassifications that reflect historical errors rather than true pathology

analogy.explanation: Just as label noise causes AI to internalize incorrect diagnostic patterns, misalignment between computational outputs and biological reasoning leads the model to produce plausible-seeming but biologically incoherent interpretations—both stem from training signals that don’t reflect real-world clinical or biological truth.

assistance_payload.scenario: An ER patient’s CT shows motion-blurred lung nodules. The researcher’s method highlights cellular activation patterns inconsistent with hypoxia physiology. Yet the expert dismisses the output not due to biological implausibility but because motion artifacts invalidate any interpretation—the input itself is untrustworthy.

aligned_path_researcher: Map the canonical sequence of steps from raw data to confirmed cell state

conflict.researcher_branch: Map the canonical sequence of steps from raw data to confirmed cell state

assistance_payload.validate_focus: Your focus on how AI filtering reduces trainees' exposure to classic cases is critically important—it directly impacts the foundational learning that shapes competent future diagnosticians.

pivot.limitation: Without a clear map of the full diagnostic workflow, we risk misidentifying which cases are truly 'classic' versus those that only appear routine due to hidden expert heuristics.
pivot.research_goal: Map the canonical sequence of steps from raw data to confirmed cell state
pivot.compelling_reason: This mapping reveals the actual decision milestones experts rely on, ensuring AI preserves—not obscures—the right educational cases for residents by aligning filtering logic with real-world discovery patterns.
pivot.coarse_scenario: On a typical morning, a resident reviews a curated set of imaging studies where AI has intentionally included exemplars tied to key diagnostic inflection points. Later, during rounds, they confidently articulate reasoning grounded in the same workflow structure their attending used—because both were trained on cases selected using a validated, expert-derived sequence.

aligned_path_expert: New grads can’t distinguish good vs. bad AI advice

conflict.expert_branch: New grads can’t distinguish good vs. bad AI advice
conflict.researcher_branch: Determine minimal validation requirements

assistance_payload.validate_focus: Your focus on new graduates' overreliance on AI advice is critically important—it highlights a real vulnerability in clinical training where inexperience can lead to uncritical acceptance of potentially flawed outputs.

pivot.limitation: Without clear, agreed-upon standards for what constitutes minimally valid AI-generated diagnostic suggestions, even well-intentioned training may inadvertently reinforce blind trust rather than calibrated judgment.
pivot.research_goal: Determine minimal validation requirements for AI-supported hypotheses, such as those involving novel cell states, to establish a credible evidence floor.
pivot.compelling_reason: This gives trainees and supervisors a shared, defensible benchmark to distinguish trustworthy AI input from speculative output—turning abstract skepticism into teachable, actionable criteria that protect both patients and learners.
pivot.coarse_scenario: In a typical morning huddle, a resident confidently questions an AI suggestion because they recognize it lacks the minimal validation markers their team now uses as standard. The attending nods in approval, knowing the trainee isn’t just doubting—but discerning—thanks to a clear, research-backed framework everyone trusts.

aligned_path_researcher: Understand criteria for 'credible' cell state claims

conflict.researcher_branch: Understand criteria for 'credible' cell state claims

probes.question: Is your concern more about reduced ability to distinguish subtle visual textures in new cases or difficulty recalling past visual patterns?
choices: Reduced ability to distinguish subtle visual textures in new cases

probes.question: Which matters more for maintaining intuition: total number of historical cases reviewed or recent case diversity?
choices: Total number of historical cases reviewed

probes.question: Is your concern related to slower initial pattern recognition or increased time to reach diagnostic confidence?
choices: Increased time to reach diagnostic confidence

probes.question: Does your diagnostic confidence erode faster when you haven’t seen a case type in 6 months or when you’ve deferred to an algorithm on similar cases recently?
choices: Haven’t seen a case type in 6 months
Deferred to an algorithm on similar cases recently

probes.question: Are you more tolerant of minor discrepancies between your intuition and the algorithm when the case is common or when it’s rare?
Which affects your intuition more: missing subtle anomalies in routine cases or overreacting to noise in complex cases?
choices: Missing subtle anomalies in routine cases
Overreacting to noise in complex cases

probes.question: Is your intuition more impaired when patient history is absent or when imaging quality is suboptimal?
Does loss of intuition correlate more strongly with rarely overriding the algorithm or frequently overriding it without feedback?

hypothetical_scenarios.scenario: If your daily exposure to raw, unprocessed diagnostic images dropped from 20 to 2 per day while algorithm use remained constant, would your judgment about 'Loss of pattern recognition intuition' change?
If your annual case volume stayed the same but all cases were of only 3 disease types instead of 30, would your judgment about 'Loss of pattern recognition intuition' change?

If your average time to form a preliminary diagnosis increased from 8 seconds to 45 seconds per case due to mandatory algorithm review, would your judgment about 'Loss of pattern recognition intuition' change?

If you hadn’t personally diagnosed a specific rare condition in 18 months but had seen 50 algorithm-confirmed cases of it during that time, would your judgment about 'Loss of pattern recognition intuition' change?

If the algorithm consistently disagreed with your intuition on benign findings (e.g., incidental lung nodules) but agreed on malignant ones, would your judgment about 'Loss of pattern recognition intuition' change?

If image preprocessing began automatically smoothing out fine-grained textural variations that you previously used to spot early disease, would your judgment about 'Loss of pattern recognition intuition' change?
If electronic health records started auto-filling clinical context using AI-generated summaries instead of physician notes, would your judgment about 'Loss of pattern recognition intuition' change?

If hospital policy required you to accept the algorithm’s output unless you could document a specific error, reducing overrides by 90%, would your judgment about 'Loss of pattern recognition intuition' change?

aligned_path_expert: Emergency vs. routine settings need different AI behavior
aligned_path_researcher: Understand how clustering resolution is selected

conflict.expert_branch: Emergency vs. routine settings need different AI behavior
conflict.researcher_branch: Understand how clustering resolution is selected

assistance_payload.validate_focus: Your focus on tailoring AI behavior to emergency versus routine clinical settings is critically important—ensuring fast triage in trauma while preserving meticulous detail in screening directly impacts patient outcomes and clinician trust.

pivot.limitation: Without a principled method for selecting how finely the AI clusters or interprets data, the system may default to a one-size-fits-all resolution that fails to adapt meaningfully between high-speed and high-precision contexts.
pivot.research_goal: Understand how clustering resolution is selected—whether guided by biological expectations, data-driven metrics, or iterative tuning—to enable context-aware adaptation.
pivot.compelling_reason: This ensures the AI doesn’t just switch modes superficially, but actually reconfigures its analytical depth to match the clinical stakes, making your speed-vs-thoroughness trade-off both reliable and defensible.
pivot.coarse_scenario: On a typical shift, the AI instantly recognizes an incoming trauma case and activates a coarse-grained, rapid-assessment mode focused on life-threatening indicators. Later, during a routine cancer screening, it seamlessly shifts to fine-grained analysis, flagging subtle anomalies with precision—because its internal resolution adapts based on validated clinical logic, not arbitrary defaults.

conflict.researcher_branch: Explore responses to non-confirmatory clusters

analogy.source_concept: Poor performance on non-Caucasian skin or body habitus

structural_mapping.inputs: Clinical images or patient data from underrepresented groups (e.g., darker skin tones, higher BMI)
structural_mapping.logic: AI model applies patterns learned from majority-group data, misinterpreting atypical presentations as low-risk or ambiguous
structural_mapping.outputs: Underdiagnosis in marginalized populations, reinforcing care gaps

analogy.explanation: Just as the AI fails on non-Caucasian skin because it lacks representative training data, the researcher’s 'non-confirmatory clusters' represent cases where evidence doesn’t match expected patterns—prompting experts to either dismiss them (risking bias) or investigate further (potentially correcting disparities). Both hinge on how systems handle inputs that deviate from dominant norms.

assistance_payload.scenario: A dermatology AI flags a dark-skinned patient’s lesion as low-risk due to training bias. The researcher’s approach would examine whether clinicians discard this output, reinterpret it, or seek confirmatory tests. The models diverge when clinicians trust the AI despite ambiguity—reinforcing disparities—while the researcher treats the case as epistemic noise rather than systemic inequity.

structural_mapping.logic: AI model trained on one manufacturer’s output fails to generalize due to unaccounted-for distributional shifts
structural_mapping.outputs: False positives or degraded performance in diagnosis

analogy.explanation: Just as switching scanner brands introduces hidden data drift that breaks model assumptions, contrast timing variations introduce temporal drift in enhancement patterns. Mapping tool strengths per task includes identifying such hidden sensitivities—so the researcher’s approach would catalog when and why the AI fails under protocol deviations, treating timing drift like hardware drift.

assistance_payload.scenario: During a liver scan, contrast arrives 5 seconds late due to IV placement issues, altering enhancement kinetics. The researcher’s method would document this as a tool limitation: the AI reliably fails under non-ideal timing. But the expert sees it as a fundamental flaw in training data scope, not just a usability quirk—the divergence peaks when the researcher treats it as a workflow issue while the expert demands retraining for real-world variability.

aligned_path_researcher: Identify the single hardest aspect of cell state discovery

conflict.researcher_branch: Identify the single hardest aspect of cell state discovery

assistance_payload.validate_focus: Your focus on managing unrealistic expectations from referring physicians—treating AI as a co-diagnoser rather than a tool—is critically important for maintaining clinical accountability and reducing defensive burden on frontline staff.

pivot.limitation: Relying solely on reactive explanations to referring physicians may not resolve the root cause: inconsistent or opaque AI outputs that fuel role confusion in the first place.
pivot.research_goal: Identify the single hardest aspect of cell state discovery—whether it’s biological interpretation, uncertainty management, iterative refinement, or tool limitations.
pivot.compelling_reason: Pinpointing the core bottleneck ensures AI outputs become more interpretable and reliable by design, directly reducing the frequency and intensity of misplaced demands for justification from colleagues.
pivot.coarse_scenario: After adoption, your morning shift begins with AI-generated insights that align intuitively with clinical reasoning—colleagues ask fewer ‘why didn’t it catch this?’ questions because the system’s behavior is predictable and grounded in transparent discovery logic. You spend less time defending tools and more time collaborating on patient care.

analogy.explanation: Just as the AI’s performance varies by body region due to training data biases, mapping tool strengths and limitations per task reveals which clinical subtasks the AI handles reliably—helping you anticipate where it might miss subtle pathologies based on task-specific blind spots.

assistance_payload.scenario: You review a trauma series where the AI flags a rib fracture but misses a tiny pneumothorax. The researcher’s approach would catalog that the tool excels at bone detection but falters on air-leak signs. The models diverge when you assume the AI’s oversight is random, while the researcher sees it as a predictable gap tied to task design.

structural_mapping.outputs: A diagnostic judgment (or hypothesis) about a potentially novel or rare condition that isn’t captured by standard labels

But when faced with a never-before-seen ultra-rare interstitial pattern, your doubt ('Is this real or noise?') clashes with the model’s assumption that all meaningful clusters follow prior heuristic templates.

assistance_payload.scenario: A radiologist sees a lung nodule that three colleagues would classify differently, warranting follow-up. assistance_payload.scenario: An MSK case shows subtle bone marrow edema where three radiologists disagree on significance. The researcher’s method assigns high reliability to spatial consistency over consensus opinion, labeling it negative. aligned_path_expert: Experienced radiologists dismiss useful AI cues

conflict.expert_branch: Experienced radiologists dismiss useful AI cues
conflict.researcher_branch: Identify unavoidable but burdensome tasks

probes.question: Is your skepticism toward AI cues more influenced by past tools that failed clinically or by tools that were merely inconvenient to use?
choices: Tools that were merely inconvenient to use

probes.question: Which matters more when evaluating AI input: maintaining independent diagnostic judgment or integrating external suggestions efficiently?
When deciding whether to trust an AI flag, do you weigh the risk of missing a critical finding more heavily than the risk of false alarms?
choices: Risk of missing a critical finding

probes.question: Would you start trusting AI output if its accuracy on subtle findings like small PEs exceeded 95% or only if it reached near-perfect (≥99%) performance?
Does your willingness to consider AI cues drop more during high-volume shifts or when interpreting unusually complex cases?
Is your resistance to AI stronger when it challenges your diagnostic authority or when it simplifies tasks you consider core to radiology expertise?
Under tight turnaround deadlines, are you more likely to ignore AI because it adds steps or because you doubt its speed-accuracy tradeoff?
Would consistent confirmation that AI correctly flagged missed findings increase your trust more than seeing real-time peer validation of AI outputs?
choices: Real-time peer validation of AI outputs

hypothetical_scenarios.scenario: If a new AI system had zero prior failures in your department over 6 months, would your judgment about 'Experienced radiologists dismiss useful AI cues' change?
If using AI required no override option and mandated acceptance of its top suggestion, would your judgment about 'Experienced radiologists dismiss useful AI cues' change?

If the AI only flagged findings with life-threatening consequences (e.g., PE, hemorrhage) and never benign anomalies, would your judgment about 'Experienced radiologists dismiss useful AI cues' change?
If the AI’s sensitivity for sub-centimeter PEs improved from 80% to 98%, would your judgment about 'Experienced radiologists dismiss useful AI cues' change?
If AI highlights appeared only after your initial read was complete—adding no concurrent distraction—would your judgment about 'Experienced radiologists dismiss useful AI cues' change?

If the AI positioned itself as a ‘second reader’ rather than a ‘decision aid,’ preserving your role as final arbiter, would your judgment about 'Experienced radiologists dismiss useful AI cues' change?
If the AI reduced total interpretation time by 20% without increasing errors, would your judgment about 'Experienced radiologists dismiss useful AI cues' change?

If every AI flag came with a timestamped audit trail showing whether it was later confirmed by follow-up imaging or clinical outcome, would your judgment about 'Experienced radiologists dismiss useful AI cues' change?
